🏛️ Decisions & Architecture

Everything you need to understand why CopilotForge works the way it does, how the pieces fit together, and what each part of the system does for you.
This guide is for end users who want to go deeper than the Quick Start without reading internal planning docs. If you just want to get going, start with Getting Started instead.

Table of Contents

  • Why This Guide Exists
  • Key Decisions
  • How the System Is Organized
  • Build Paths — A through J
  • What Gets Generated (and Where)
  • CLI Command Map
  • Code Map — What Each File Does
  • Validation and Safety
  • Decision Timeline
  • Further Reading

Why This Guide Exists

CopilotForge is deliberately simple to use — one command and a few questions. But that simplicity rests on a set of deliberate architecture choices. This guide explains those choices in plain language so you can:
  • Understand why the wizard generates specific files
  • Know what happens behind the scenes when you pick a build path
  • Feel confident customizing the output because you understand the structure
  • Contribute or report issues with context about how things connect

Key Decisions

These are the foundational choices that shape everything CopilotForge does.

1. Zero Dependencies

| Decision | The entire framework — skills, agents, memory — is plain Markdown. Nothing to install, nothing to break. | |----------|:---| | Why | You should be able to drop CopilotForge into any repo (Python, Go, Rust, an empty folder) without adding a single runtime dependency. Node.js is only needed to run the npx copilotforge init command itself. | | What this means for you | The files CopilotForge creates have no lock files, no node_modules, no build step. You can read, edit, or delete any of them. |

2. Markdown as the Universal Format

| Decision | Every skill, agent, and memory file is a .md file that any AI assistant can read. | |----------|:---| | Why | GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, Cursor, and ChatGPT all understand Markdown. A .md instruction file works everywhere without format-specific tooling. | | What this means for you | You are never locked into one AI vendor. Switch assistants and the skills still work. |

3. Memory Lives in Your Repo

| Decision | Decisions, patterns, and preferences are stored as plain-text files in forge-memory/ inside your project. | |----------|:---| | Why | Nothing is sent to external servers. You own the data. You can read it, edit it, or add forge-memory/ to .gitignore if you don't want it in version control. | | What this means for you | Your AI assistant gets smarter over time because it reads the same memory files each session. Delete them to start fresh at any time. |

4. Skip-on-Exist Safety

| Decision | The init command never overwrites files that already exist unless you explicitly confirm. | |----------|:---| | Why | You might have customized a skill, written decisions into memory, or edited FORGE.md. Overwriting without asking would destroy your work. | | What this means for you | Running init twice is safe. It creates missing files and skips existing ones. Use --yes to auto-overwrite when you know what you're doing. |

5. Ten Build Paths

| Decision | Projects are classified into one of ten paths (A through J) that determine which files are generated. | |----------|:---| | Why | A Power BI report and a Next.js API need completely different skills, agents, and recipes. One-size-fits-all generation produced irrelevant output for specialized projects. | | What this means for you | The wizard silently detects your path from your project description. You get only the files relevant to your project — no noise, no extras to delete. |

6. User-Owned Files Are Sacred

| Decision | CopilotForge will never modify forge-memory/ contents, your root FORGE.md, or files in cli/files/ during upgrades. | |----------|:---| | Why | These files contain your decisions, your project state, and scaffolded assets that existing users depend on. Touching them risks data loss or broken setups. | | What this means for you | The upgrade command updates framework skills and recipes but leaves everything you've written untouched. |

How the System Is Organized

CopilotForge has six layers. Each layer has a clear job and they connect in one direction: the CLI triggers skills, skills activate agents, and agents produce output files. `` You (or your AI assistant) │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ CLI │ npx copilotforge init / doctor / status │ cli/bin/copilotforge.js │ └─────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ Skills │ .github/skills/{name}/SKILL.md │ (Instruction files for AI) │ The Planner, path guides, code review, testing └─────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ Agents │ .copilot/agents/{name}.md │ (AI team members) │ Planner, Reviewer, Tester, path-specific agents └─────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────┬─────────────┬────────┐ │ Memory │ Cookbook │FORGE.md│ │forge-mem/│ cookbook/ │ (root) │ └──────────┴─────────────┴────────┘ `
Skills are the "how" — written instructions for a specific task.
Agents are the "who" — team members that know which skills to use.
Memory is the "what we decided" — a persistent record of choices.
Cookbook is the "show me the code" — runnable examples for your stack.
FORGE.md is the "control panel" — one file showing the full picture.
For a deeper dive into each layer, see How It Works.

Build Paths — A through J

When you answer the wizard's first question ("What are you building?"), CopilotForge silently classifies your project into one of ten paths. Paths A–I are for Power Platform projects; Path J is the general developer path for everything else. | Path | Name | What It's For | Key Tools | |------|------|---------------|-----------| | A | Copilot Studio Agent | No-code conversational agents | Copilot Studio | | B | Studio + Connector | Agents that call external APIs | Power Automate, REST APIs | | C | Declarative Agent | Microsoft 365 Copilot agents | Teams Toolkit, VS Code | | D | Canvas App Agent | No-code/low-code apps with AI | Power Apps, Power Fx | | E | Power Automate | Automated workflows with AI | Power Automate, Dataverse | | F | PCF Component | Custom UI components (TypeScript) | Node 16+, pac CLI | | G | Power BI Report | Data reports and models | Power BI Desktop, DAX | | H | SharePoint Agent | Knowledge agents for Teams | SharePoint, M365 Copilot | | I | Power Pages | Websites with AI plugins | Power Pages Studio | | J | Developer Project | General coding projects | git, Node.js, any stack |

How Path Detection Works

1. You describe your project in the wizard (Q1) 2. The forge-compass classifier scans your description for keywords (e.g., "canvas app," "PCF," "Power BI") 3. If Power Platform signals are detected, you get 3 short follow-up questions to narrow the path 4. If no signals are detected, you're silently routed to Path J with no interruption 5. Your path is written into FORGE.md as a stamp:

How Paths Change What You Get

Each path generates a tailored set of files:
  • FORGE.md — Customized project summary, team roster, and skill index for your path
  • Skills — Only the skills relevant to your path (e.g., Path F gets pcf-component, Path A gets studio-agent)
  • Agents — Path-specific agents that know your tools (e.g., Path G gets a Power BI architect agent)
  • Cookbook — Only recipes that apply (e.g., Path F gets a PCF TypeScript skeleton; Path E gets flow patterns)
Path J users — If you're building a general dev project (API, web app, CLI tool, etc.), nothing changes from the standard experience. Path J is the default and produces the same output as previous versions.
Want help choosing a path? See What Should I Use? for a decision flowchart.

What Gets Generated (and Where)

When you run npx copilotforge init, these files are created in your project:

Always Created (Core)

| File | Location | Purpose | |------|----------|---------| | Planner skill | .github/skills/planner/SKILL.md | The wizard — the AI reads this to run your setup | | Planner reference | .github/skills/planner/reference.md | Format spec and re-run rules for the wizard | | Plan-executor skill | .github/skills/plan-executor/SKILL.md | Powers The Ralph Loop (autonomous task execution) | | Plan-executor reference | .github/skills/plan-executor/reference.md | Format spec for the plan executor |

Created on Full Install (Default)

| File | Location | Purpose | |------|----------|---------| | FORGE.md | repo root | Your project's control panel — shows everything that was set up | | Implementation plan | IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md | Task list for The Ralph Loop | | Planner agent | .copilot/agents/planner.md | AI team member that runs the wizard | | Memory files | forge-memory/decisions.md, patterns.md, preferences.md | Decision log, conventions, user settings | | Starter recipes | cookbook/hello-world.ts, cookbook/hello-world.py | Minimal runnable examples | | Task loop | cookbook/task-loop.ts or .py | The Ralph Loop engine | | Getting started | docs/GETTING-STARTED.md | Quick-start guide for your project |

Created for Power Platform Paths (A–I)

| What | Example | |------|---------| | Path-specific skills | .github/skills/pcf-component/SKILL.md (Path F) | | Path-specific agents | .copilot/agents/pcf-agent.md (Path F) | | Path-specific recipes | cookbook/pcf-component.ts, cookbook/pcf-manifest.md (Path F) | | Platform guides | cookbook/copilot-studio-guide.md (Path A) |
Minimal install (--minimal): Creates only the 2 core planner files. Use this when you want the wizard without any generated output.

CLI Command Map

Every command CopilotForge provides, what it does, and when to use it. | Command | What It Does | When to Use It | |---------|-------------|----------------| | npx copilotforge init | Scaffolds all files into your project | First-time setup | | npx copilotforge init --minimal | Creates planner skill only (2 files) | Lightweight setup | | npx copilotforge init --yes | Auto-overwrite, auto-commit, no prompts | CI/scripting, /yolo mode | | npx copilotforge init --dry-run | Shows what would be created without writing | Preview before committing | | npx copilotforge init --beginner | Adds extra explanation files alongside every skill | New to AI coding tools | | npx copilotforge doctor | Health check — verifies files, versions, git config | Troubleshooting, after upgrades | | npx copilotforge doctor --json | Machine-readable health check output | CI pipelines | | npx copilotforge status | Terminal dashboard — plan, memory, skills, git | Quick project overview | | npx copilotforge dashboard | Browser dashboard at localhost:3731 | Visual overview with Ralph Loop controls | | npx copilotforge upgrade | Updates framework-owned files, preserves your content | After a new CopilotForge release | | npx copilotforge upgrade --dry-run | Shows what would change without touching files | Preview before upgrading | | npx copilotforge watch | Runs The Ralph Loop continuously (polls every 10s) | Autonomous task execution | | npx copilotforge run | Runs through pending tasks once, then exits | One-shot task execution | | npx copilotforge uninstall | Removes CLI-installed skill files only | Clean removal | | npx copilotforge (no args) | Interactive terminal menu | Exploring commands |

Code Map — What Each File Does

If you want to understand the codebase itself, here's what each part does:

Entry Point

| File | Purpose | |------|---------| | cli/bin/copilotforge.js | Routes command-line arguments to the right module. Handles --help, --version, and all subcommands. |

Command Modules (cli/src/)

| Module | Responsibility | |--------|---------------| | init.js | Copies core skill files from cli/files/, writes template files from templates/, offers git commit | | doctor.js | Runs 10+ health checks: Node version, file existence, git config, write permissions, version stamp, path-specific prerequisites | | status.js | Reads plan, memory, skills, agents, cookbook, and git data — exports functions for the dashboard views | | dashboard.js | Starts a local web server at port 3731, serves a live HTML dashboard with auto-refresh | | watch.js | The Ralph Loop's persistent runner — polls for pending tasks and invokes them | | run.js | One-shot task runner — processes pending tasks once and exits | | interactive.js | Readline-based terminal menu that loops until you choose Exit | | upgrade.js | Diffs framework files against disk, updates only what changed, never touches user-owned files | | uninstall.js | Removes CLI-installed planner skill files and cleans up empty directories | | utils.js | Shared helpers: terminal colors, user prompts, file I/O, git operations |

Template System (cli/src/templates/)

Templates generate the files that get written into your project. They are split into focused modules: | Module | What It Generates | |--------|------------------| | forge.js | Generic FORGE.md (Path J / default) | | platform-forge.js | 9 path-specific FORGE.md variants (Paths A–I) | | agents.js | Agent definition markdown | | memory.js | forge-memory/ file templates | | cookbook.js | Cookbook recipe templates | | init.js | Init-phase file templates | | platform-guides.js | Power Platform guide content | | index.js | Barrel file — re-exports all template modules |

Source Files (Read-Only, Not Generated)

| Location | What It Contains | |----------|-----------------| | cli/files/ | Exact copies of skill files shipped to users — these are never modified by upgrades | | .github/skills/ | The authoritative skill definitions (planner, plan-executor, path skills) | | .copilot/agents/ | Internal agent definitions used by the planner's delegation system |

Validation and Safety

CopilotForge has built-in safeguards that protect you and your project.

Files That Are Never Overwritten

These files belong to you. CopilotForge will never modify them during init or upgrade: | File/Folder | Why It's Protected | |-------------|-------------------| | forge-memory/ | Contains your decisions, patterns, and preferences | | FORGE.md (project root) | Your project's control panel — you may have customized it | | cli/files/ | Shipped verbatim to users — changes would break existing installs |

Health Checks (doctor)

Run npx copilotforge doctor to verify your setup. It checks:
  • Node.js version (18+ required)
  • Required files exist (SKILL.md, reference.md, FORGE.md)
  • Git is configured (user.name, user.email)
  • Write permissions on target directories
  • Version stamp matches the installed CLI version
  • Path-specific checks (e.g., Path F verifies Node 16+, pac CLI availability)

Version Stamps

Every generated FORGE.md includes a version stamp: `html ` The doctor command reads these stamps to detect outdated installs or missing path information. If you see a version mismatch warning, run npx copilotforge upgrade.

User-Facing Content Rules

All generated files, templates, and documentation follow these rules:
  • No internal jargon — Implementation-level vocabulary is kept out of user-facing output
  • Beginner-friendly — Technical terms are explained on first use
  • Copy-pasteable — Every code example runs without modification
  • Self-contained — No file requires reading another file to make sense

Testing Before Contributions

If you're contributing to CopilotForge, the repo includes automated checks: | Check | Command | What It Validates | |-------|---------|-------------------| | Unit tests | node --test cli/tests/*.test.js | CLI module behavior | | Content scan | node tests/e2e-validate.js | No internal terms leaking into user-facing files | | Scaffold test | pwsh tests/validate-scaffold.ps1 | Generated files are valid |

Decision Timeline

A summary of how CopilotForge evolved, showing the key decisions at each stage. | Version | Codename | Key Decisions | |---------|----------|---------------| | 0.1.0 | Phase 1 — Planner Skill | Markdown-only skills, trigger-phrase activation, template system | | 0.2.0 | Phase 2 — Wizard + Agents | 5-question wizard, agent delegation, internal templates | | 0.3.0 | Phase 3 — Cookbook | 12 recipe pairs (TS + Python), stack detection from manifest files, FORGE.md live config | | 0.4.0 | Phase 4 — Memory | Read-write learning loop, adaptive wizard for returning users, convention extraction | | 0.5.0 | Phase 5 — Beginner Polish | Standalone FAQ, cross-platform docs, expanded stack detection, GitHub templates | | 1.4.0 | --yes Flag | Autonomous mode for CI/scripting, /yolo detection | | 1.5.0 | Memory Conflicts | Memory conflict detection, forge remember:` feedback loop, persistent experience level, The Ralph Loop branding, doctor expansion | | 1.6.0 | Phase 13 — Path Awareness | 10 build paths (A–J), forge-compass classifier, 9 path-specific skills, 6 Power Platform agent templates, 16 cookbook recipes, path-stamp system, path-aware doctor checks | | Next | Phase 14 — Wizard & Distribution (planned) | Zero-arg conversational wizard, snapshot rollback, MCP server for multi-client distribution, examples gallery, typed plugin API |
The full changelog is in CHANGELOG.md.

Further Reading

This guide synthesizes decisions from the implementation plan, changelog, and system design docs into one place. For the raw planning history, see IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md and CHANGELOG.md.
